pattern: To fit as many distinct integers as possible under a sum cap, greedily taking the smallest still-available integers first never does worse than any other selection of the same size.
hints: Think about which integers you would regret choosing least: the smallest ones cost the least sum per integer taken.
Walk the range from 1 upward, skip anything banned, and keep adding an integer to your running sum as long as it still fits under maxSum.
Stop the walk the moment the next available integer would push the running sum over maxSum -- the count you've accumulated so far is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: banned list contains a value above n, which should simply be ignored
banned list has duplicate entries for the same value
smallest possible maxSum forces at most one integer chosen
n equals 1 and it is banned, nothing can be chosen
n equals 1 and it is available
maxSum large enough for every unbanned integer in range
every integer in range is banned

stress.description: random banned subset of a random-size range with a random sum cap
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 10000))
    ban_count = rng.randint(0, n)
    banned = rng.sample(range(1, n + 1), ban_count) if ban_count else [rng.randint(1, 10000)]
    max_sum = rng.randint(1, 1000000000)
    return [banned, n, max_sum]
